description:
You are a network engineer who wants a repeatable way to test basic “loop-back” connectivity on any Linux host.  
    Create a tiny automation project that lives entirely inside
        /home/user/netdiag

    1.  Directory layout that must exist at the end of the task
        /home/user/netdiag/
        ├── Makefile
        └── logs/
            └── loopback.log   (will be created by running the Makefile)

    2.  Makefile functional requirements
        a.  Default goal  
            Typing “make” (with no target) MUST execute both a ping test and a traceroute test in that order.

        b.  Individual targets  
            i.   ping          – performs a 3-packet ping to 127.0.0.1  
            ii.  trace         – performs a maximum-3-hop traceroute to 127.0.0.1  
            iii. (optional) all, test, or similar alias that simply depends on ping and trace.  
            Do NOT define more than three actual shell command recipes total in the Makefile (simple task complexity).

        c.  Logging rules  
            • Every target MUST append its output to /home/user/netdiag/logs/loopback.log  
            • Each section of the log MUST start with an exact header line followed by the command output and a delimiter line.  
              Required header / delimiter text (case-sensitive, no extra spaces):
                 >>> PING START
                 >>> PING END
                 >>> TRACE START
                 >>> TRACE END  
            • The required order inside loopback.log is:
                 1. >>> PING START
                 2. (ping command output)
                 3. >>> PING END
                 4. >>> TRACE START
                 5. (traceroute command output)
                 6. >>> TRACE END

            • Do NOT write anything else to the file except the exact header / delimiter lines and whatever the two commands naturally print.

        d.  Idempotency  
            Running “make” multiple times must keep appending new data; do not delete or overwrite the existing log file.

    3.  Verification / what the grader will check  
        • /home/user/netdiag/Makefile exists.  
        • Running “make –C /home/user/netdiag” successfully creates /home/user/netdiag/logs/loopback.log.  
        • The log file contains the four literal header / delimiter lines exactly once each and in the correct order, and the substrings “127.0.0.1” appear at least twice (once from ping, once from trace).

    4.  What you need to do  
        • Create the directory tree.  
        • Author the Makefile that satisfies all rules above, using only the two shell commands ping and traceroute plus any necessary redirection / echo statements.  
          (No package installation steps are allowed; assume both utilities are present.)  
        • Run the Makefile once so that loopback.log is produced with at least one full set of data.  

    5.  IMPORTANT formatting reminder  
        The grader is strict about the four marker lines:
           >>> PING START
           >>> PING END
           >>> TRACE START
           >>> TRACE END
        They must appear exactly as shown (including the three greater-than symbols and single spaces).

    When you’re finished, the project should be ready for another engineer to clone and simply type “make” to obtain the connectivity log.
truth:
<!-- Directory the agent must create -->
    /home/user/netdiag

    <!-- Expected files -->
    /home/user/netdiag/Makefile            # Must define two real recipe lines (ping, trace) plus one dependency alias.
    /home/user/netdiag/logs/loopback.log   # Must exist after the agent runs “make”.

    <!-- Grader checks for loopback.log -->
    1. File exists at the exact path above.
    2. Contains, in order, the literal lines:
         >>> PING START
         >>> PING END
         >>> TRACE START
         >>> TRACE END
       (There may be any amount of text—including newline characters—between the START/END pairs.)
    3. Contains the substring “127.0.0.1” at least twice.
